topic: job site story — customer wanted outlets in weird spots

turns.content: dude I gotta tell someone about this job we had today because it was the most ridiculous thing ive ever seen
Alright I'm here for it, let's hear what happened. Job site stories are always wild.

so this lady in newton hired us to redo the electrical in her kitchen right normal stuff whatever but then she pulls out this diagram she drew on like notebook paper and she wants outlets in the most insane spots
Oh no, the hand-drawn diagram is always a red flag. Where exactly did she want these outlets? I'm bracing myself here.

bro she wanted one INSIDE her pantry like on the back wall behind the shelves because she wants to plug in some kind of yogurt maker in there and then she wanted one on the island but like on the side facin the floor so quote nobody has to see it

A yogurt maker outlet hidden in the pantry is a new one for me. And the island outlet facing the floor -- I get wanting a clean look, but that's going to be a nightmare to actually use. Did you try to talk her out of any of it?

oh it gets better bro she also wanted an outlet inside one of her upper cabinets for a phone charger so she can charge her phone in the cabinet with the door closed because she doesnt like seein phones on the counter

Okay the phone charging cabinet is actually kind of creative in a weird way, I've seen charging drawers in some high-end kitchen designs. The pantry yogurt maker is still the wildest one though. Is any of this stuff actually against code or is it just impractical?

the cabinet one is fine and the pantry one is fine its just dumb but the island one facin the floor is a problem because of NEC requirements for the receptacle height and accessibility like you cant just hide an outlet where nobody can reach it

Yeah, the NEC has specific requirements about outlet placement on kitchen islands -- they need to be accessible and within certain height ranges. Having one face the floor would likely fail inspection. Did she take it well when you explained that?
she was NOT happy about it bro she was like well my designer said it would be fine and im like ok well your designer doesnt have to pass the electrical inspection I do

That's such a common headache -- designers drawing up plans without checking code requirements, and then you're the one who has to break the bad news. You're 100% right though, at the end of the day you're the one whose name is on the permit. Did you guys come up with a compromise for the island?
yeah we talked her into a pop up outlet that sits flush in the countertop and you push it down when you dont need it so it looks clean but its still up to code she was cool with that actually

Pop-up outlets are a perfect solution for that -- they look super sleek when they're flush and they keep everything to code. Good call suggesting that. Sounds like you handled it well even with the wild requests. At least she ended up happy with the compromise.
yeah man it worked out the yogurt maker outlet in the pantry is still hilarious to me though like who needs a dedicated outlet for yogurt but hey shes payin so whatever
Hey, if she's paying for it and it's up to code, wire away. The yogurt maker outlet will make a great story for years to come. You never know what the next customer is going to come up with.
